chunk_summary: This section outlines protein quantity and quality guidelines for children, emphasizing age-specific daily targets, amino acid balance, and dietary strategies for growth, obesity, and clinical exceptions.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific protein requirements for children from infancy to adolescence.
Learn strategies to ensure high-quality protein intake in cereal-dominant diets.
Recognize clinical scenarios requiring adjusted protein density, such as preterm infants or chronic kidney disease.
Identify practical approaches to balance protein intake in cases of growth faltering or obesity.
key_facts: Daily protein targets: 0–6 months: 8 g, 6–12 months: 10.5 g, 1–3 years: 12.5 g, 4–6 years: 16 g, 7–9 years: 23 g, 10–12 years: 32 g, late adolescence: 46 g/day for girls, 55 g/day for boys.
Convert grams/day to g/kg/day using the child’s weight to avoid overprescription or underrecognition of deficits.
High-quality protein sources include milk, curd, paneer, egg, fish, chicken, or vegetarian combinations like rice with dal and roti with chana.
Growth faltering requires balanced energy plus protein, avoiding reliance on protein powders.
In obesity, protein intake should be maintained while reducing free sugars and ultra-processed snacks.
Clinical exceptions include higher protein density for preterm infants and specialist targets for chronic kidney disease.
reference_quote: Convert grams/day to g/kg/day using the child’s current weight; this prevents overprescribing protein in small children and underrecognizing deficits in adolescents with low body weight. (Source: ICMR-NIN 2020 brief note)
document_key_topics: Strategies for high-quality protein intake in cereal-dominant diets
Protein considerations in growth faltering and obesity
Clinical exceptions for protein density in special populations
